coredata.dc:title: A facile and green synthesis of CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 nanohybrid for recyclable and enhanced photocatalysis in dye degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Kai
Zhang, Tianshu
Huang, Lunjie
Suo, Yourui
Zhang, Daohong
Sun, Jing
Wang, Jianlong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we demonstrate a facile synthesis of CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 nanohybrid composites as an efficient photocatalyst to enhance the removal of dyes from wastewater. Detailed characterization through SEM, TEM, EDAS, UV-vis, XRD, FT-IR and VSM confirmed the synthesis of thin MoS2 nanosheets and the formation of the nanohybrid composites. As a result, CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 exhibits excellent reusability through magnetic separation and its degradation rate of methylene blue as high as 96.8% with few additions of materials (25 μg/ml). The ultrahigh photocatalytic performance and chemical stability, accompanied with this facile and simple synthetic process, promising CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 as a low-cost, ultrahigh-performance and robust photocatalyst in practical dyes degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite materials
MoS2 nanosheets
Carbon dots
Reusability
Solar energy materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of layered MoS2
Morphology and compositions of the as-prepared photocatalysts
Mechanism for the highly improved photocatalytic activity of CMF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV-vis spectrum of MoS2 nanosheets. (B) SEM image of MoS2. (C) TEM image of MoS2. (D) SEM image of G-MoS2 and the corresponding elemental mappings. (E) UV/Vis absorption and (F) determination of the bandgap energies of bulk MoS2 and MoS2 nanosheets.
SEM images of nanohybrid composites at low (A) and magnified (B) scale. (C) XRD patterns of nanohybrid composites and MoS2. (D) FT-IR spectra of nanohybrid composites and MoS2.
(A) Catalytic mechanism. (B) Magnetization curves of CMF. (C) The degradation for MB with different materials. (D) Four repeated processes with nanohybrids.

head.ce:title: A facile and green synthesis of CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 nanohybrid for recyclable and enhanced photocatalysis in dye degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources, Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810008, Qinghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qinghai Key Laboratory of Qinghai-Tibet Plateau Biological Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China (J. Wang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we demonstrate a facile synthesis of CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 nanohybrid composites as an efficient photocatalyst to enhance the removal of dyes from wastewater. Detailed characterization through SEM, TEM, EDAS, UV-vis, XRD, FT-IR and VSM confirmed the synthesis of thin MoS2 nanosheets and the formation of the nanohybrid composites. As a result, CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 exhibits excellent reusability through magnetic separation and its degradation rate of methylene blue as high as 96.8% with few additions of materials (25 μg/ml). The ultrahigh photocatalytic performance and chemical stability, accompanied with this facile and simple synthetic process, promising CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 as a low-cost, ultrahigh-performance and robust photocatalyst in practical dyes degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: When exfoliated into one or a limited number of layers, 2D MoS2 emerges an improved photocatalysis property due to the transformation of indirect bandgap to direct bandgap [1–3]. Chemical exfoliation and solvothermal synthesis are common methods for the fabrication of MoS2 nanosheets. Multiple heaping may expose more inert basal planes than active edges, leading to the loss of catalytical activity. Additionally, without appropriate immobilization, powdered nanomaterials will suffer from the particle aggragation, which hinders their potential applications due to the degradation in their optical, electronic, and catalytic properties [4,5]. Therefore, some strategies have been devoted to modify MoS2 with active biomolecules or reassemble complexes with MoS2 to form heterostructure compounds [6,7]. Nevertheless, most of the efforts seem to have been hampered by specialized equipment or noxious solvents with unsustainable and environmentally unfriendly process as a result of multiple and complex steps.

This was demonstrated in a number of studies that photoactive carbon dots (CDs) can serve as photogenerated electron acceptors or donors. However, the recycling of substrate-free CDs demands additional separation steps after the reaction, which hinders their potential in practical application. In this study, glucose-assisted grinding was utilized to exfoliate and modify MoS2 for the facile fabrication of CDs modified MoS2 nanohybrids through dehydrating glucose in situ on the surface of MoS2. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were composited as well to provide magnetic recycling and synergetic co-catalystic activity [8,9].
The detailed synthesis and characterization processes are available in ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of layered MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, glucose assisted grinding was applied to one-step exfoliate and functionalize MoS2 nanosheets for large-scale synthesis. The typical morphologies and structures of the G-MoS2 nanosheets were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in Fig. 1 B. The shear force derived from grinding glucose significantly facilitated the decrease of the material size from the bulk to the thin layer. To further ascertain the nature of the dispersed material, we performed transmission electron microscopy (TEM), with typical images shown in Fig. 1C. It clearly shows that the MoS2 nanosheets have a clear thin lateral dimensions, indicating MoS2 was exfoliated to layered sheets successfully [10].

Four characteristic absorption peaks located at 683, 623, 482 and 411 nm are observed using a UV–vis absorption measurement (Fig. 1A). The direct excitonic transition from the deep valence to the conduction band results in absorption bands at 406 and 472 nm; the absorption peaks at 619 and 679 nm can be attributed to the excitonic transitions from the K point of the Brillouin zone, indicating an increase in the bandgap of the nanoscale MoS2. EDAX analysis were conducted. Fig. 1D revealed that C element is uniformly distributed among the nanohybrids, which verifies that glucose was dispersed homogeneously on the MoS2 surface. As proven by UV/Vis absorption curves and Kubelka–Munk plots in Fig. 1E and F, the bandgap of bulk MoS2 and MoS2 nanosheets were about 2.27 eV and 2.05 eV, respectively. In comparison to bulk MoS2, the MoS2 nanosheets show a narrower band gap, which means its thinner layers with the exfoliation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and compositions of the as-prepared photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and structures of as-synthesized CDs-Fe3O4-MoS2 nanohybrids were observed by SEM. The low magnification image in Fig. 2 A shows that the nanohybrid composites display standard nanosheets structures after solvothermal process, indicating the high uniformity in structure. As shown in Fig. 2B, the magnified image of obtained material proves the banding of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and MoS2 nanosheets.

The XRD pattern of pure MoS2 and CMF nanohybrids are shown in Fig. 2C. For pure MoS2, four characteristic XRD peaks of 100, 103, 105, and 008 were observed, which are ascribed to MoS2 crystals. For CMF nanohybrid composites, the same four diffraction peaks are also observed and reflections of 220, 311, 400, 422, 511, and 440 can be indexed to the magnetic Fe3O4 nanocrystals [11,12]. Additionally, the additional XRD peak at 2θ = 23° relates to the carbon phase after the carbonization of glucose. FT-IR spectroscopy of MoS2 and obtained composite were performed in range from 4000 to 400 cm-1. As can be seen from Fig. 2D, CMF nanocomposites produced very semblable absorption pattern for pure MoS2. Moreover, some absorption peak difference can be observed in the CMF sample. The characteristic absorption peak at 1710 cm-1 and 1042 cm-1 in the CMF sample is related to the C=O and C–O–C stretching vibration. The enhanced and shifted peak at 3408 cm-1 with –OH groups further may result from abundant active groups on CDs [13,14].

We investigate the magnetic properties of CMF using the magnetometer. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the value of MS is 41.47 emu·g-1 for CMF. No coercivity or remanence was observed at room temperature from the hysteresis loop, indicating the superparamagnetic behavior of the nanohybrids [15]. Therefore, these nanohybrid composites are satisfactory for separation and recycling in practical industry as a result of its strong magnetism with weak magnetic fields.

To compare the photocatalytic activity of bulk MoS2, layered MoS2 and CDs-Fe3O4-MoS2 composites, a range of photodegradation experiments were implemented. Fig. 3C shows the photocatalysis of MB as a function of time by bulk MoS2, layered MoS2 and CMF under sunlight irradiation. In comparison, layered MoS2 nanosheets partially bleached methylene blue while nonexfoliated MoS2 only bleached methylene blue in limited level. Meanwhile, for CMF nanohybrids, the photocatalyst can decompose about 96.8% of MB at 15 min, is about 4.7 times as high as that of the bulk MoS2 in same irradiation time. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics (k) of MB in the existence of different materials were also investigated (Fig. S1). To study the photocatalytic stability and recycling ability of the prepared CMF photocatalysts, four repetitive reaction cycles for the degradation of MB have been conducted. After the first cycle, the efficiency of removal for MB is 96.8%, slightly lesser than that of raw materials. The nanohybrid composites exhibit an outstanding photostability as the decomposition percentage of MB only decreases slightly after every recycling, which can be ascribed to the limited loss of materials during the recycling procedure with magnetic separation, water washing, drying, and redispersion in solution. However, the decomposition percentage of MB based on the photocatalysts still reached 94.5% after four runs. The reusability tests of CMF reveal its satisfying stability with negligible activity reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the highly improved photocatalytic activity of CMF
ce:section.ce:para: The probable mechanism for the photocatalysis decomposition of MB by the CMF nanohybrids shows in Fig. 3A. The electrons in the valence band (VB) can easily transfer to the conduction band (CB) of Fe3O4 owing to the shorter wavelength light converted by CDs. In addition, they may also immediately transfer to the VB of MoS2 through the intimate contacts because of the Fe3O4/MoS2/Fe3O4 sandwich-like heterojunction nanostructure. These electrons then jump to the CB of MoS2 and transfer to that of Fe3O4 and CDs. When the CMF nanohybrids were exposed to sunlight irradiation, the light with a shorter wavelength will be emitted because of the upconversion PL capacity of the CDs. Such shorter wavelength light can effectively excite the Fe3O4 nanoparticles to produce electron-hole (e--h+) pairs. In addition, these electrons may also immediately transfer to MoS2 through the intimate contacts because of the Fe3O4/MoS2/Fe3O4 sandwich-like heterojunction nanostructure. These electron transitions can effectively restrain the recombination of electrons-holes so that lengthen the charge lifetime. The holes and electrons react with OH– or oxygen molecules, generating hydroxyl radicals (̇OH) and superoxide anion radicals (̇O2 –), respectively. Such ̇OH and ̇O2 – radicals with potent oxidation capacity can efficiently degradate organic pollutants.

In conclusion, superior CDs-MoS2-Fe3O4 nanohybrid composites were successfully fabricated by one pot solvothermal method. Detailed characterization through SEM, TEM, EDAS, UV-vis, XRD, FT-IR and VSM confirmed the synthesis of thin MoS2 nanosheets and the formation of the nanohybrid composites. The prepared nanohybrids exhibit high activity for removing methyl blue in the visible-light-active photocatalysis degradation process. The nanohybrids can be effortlessly recovered by an external magnet for recycling use. Overall, the convenience of synthesis and functionalization of nanohybrids by this method may pave a way to of MoS2-based composites and expand their applications in practical dyes degradation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financed by Grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675127), Capacity Building Project of Engineering Research Center of Qinghai Province (2017-GX-G03) and the Development Project of Qinghai Key Laboratory (2017-ZJ-Y10) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2018.08.106.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
